title: Dog Ingested Peeling Paint Chips: Risk Assessment and Next Steps
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your dog eats paint chips. Learn about lead poisoning risks, when to see a vet, and why photos help with triage.
content_html:
What Happens When a Dog Eats Paint Chips?

When a dog ingests peeling paint chips, the risk level depends largely on the age of the paint and the quantity consumed. Modern household paints are typically latex or acrylic-based and are generally non-toxic, though they can cause mild stomach upset. However, the most significant danger comes from older homes (built before 1978 in the US) which may contain lead-based paint. If ingested, lead can be absorbed into the bloodstream, leading to serious neurological and gastrointestinal issues.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. If your dog has ingested paint chips, you should contact a veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately for guidance. While it may not require an emergency room visit if your dog is acting normally, lead poisoning is a cumulative toxin. If your dog exhibits symptoms such as vomiting, seizures, extreme lethargy, or uncoordinated movement, the situation upgrades to high urgency and requires immediate veterinary intervention.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a photo of the area where the paint was consumed, as well as a photo of the chips themselves, is a vital part of the triage process. A veterinarian can use these images to assess the texture, thickness, and number of layers in the paint. This helps them determine the likelihood of the paint being an older, lead-based variety and allows them to provide more accurate advice on whether you need to come in for blood testing or decontamination.

faqs.question: Signs include vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, tremors, seizures, and behavioral changes like aggression or anxiety.
Modern paints are usually non-toxic but can still cause mild digestive upset or, in rare cases, a physical blockage if a large amount of dried material is swallowed.
Do not induce vomiting at home unless specifically directed to do so by a veterinary professional, as this can sometimes lead to aspiration pneumonia.
